utterances: And they tallied it up.
And among these 2,000 households, they found more than 600 deaths.
Well, Richard, maybe the key question is how many of these deaths are attributable to the war?
That is, what was the death rate before the war, and how does this number compare?
And that's an excellent question.
And what they did was they looked at the death rate in the year before the war, and then they looked at the death rate in the three years thereafter.

And what they did was they extrapolated from these 600 known deaths across the entire Iraqi population and they came up with a number of 650,000 excess deaths, deaths that would not have happened had the war not happened.
Now these are deaths that are pretty directly attributable to the war, you're saying, but they're not necessarily people who all die in combat.
These are - there's some increase in disease, heart disease and other diseases that may be attributable to the war.
Right.
This is everything.
And it includes combat deaths as well, because many, many of these people who died were young men of military age.
And the surveyors were not able to figure out exactly whether somebody died in sectarian violence or in combat with the U.S.
and coalition troops and so on.
But what they did find was that most of the excess deaths were violent deaths.
And many of those violent deaths were gunshots or car bombs or other explosions or air strikes.
Now these researchers have said that they have documentary evidence.
They have death certificates to support their research in these 2,000 households.
At least in many cases they do.
Still, they must know that this is going to be a very controversial figure.
It's so much greater than what had been reported earlier.
Well, it is true that two years ago, they came up with a number 100,000 using very similar methods - a smaller sample.
And that was attacked viciously.
It was right before the election again, and so they were saying this is politically motivated.
Well, this time it's a much bigger survey.
And there is less uncertainty, although I should emphasize that this is 650,000 plus or minus a few hundred thousand.
So it could be actually considerably worse than this or not quite as bad.
But still, hundreds and hundreds of thousands of deaths is what their study suggests.
I asked Gilbert Burnham, who is the primary author on this study - you know, once again you're right before the election, you're making this announcement.
Is this politically motivated?
And he said, no, it's not politically motivated.
But then he did go on to say that he did press to get this published quickly in The Lancet because he said this is an important topic that needed public discussion.
Does the NPR science desk think this is a worthy study?
Dorjee was stunned.
He wiped away tears as the theater erupted into a standing ovation.
Meanwhile, the family business, the Himalaya restaurant, has been packed lately.
On a Tuesday night, almost every table was taken.
At the bar, Catherine Eloranto was signing a giant card that said we are a nation of immigrants.
It's wonderful to see so many people coming out and supporting him.
Someone also wrote love zone in chalk on the sidewalk outside.
Dorjee said he felt overwhelmed and humbled by the community response, and now the idea of getting a gun just seems stupid.
When I look at that kind of support, then I do not feel the need to bear any kind of firearms because that is my firearm.
While Dorjee has been embraced in a big way, some people in this part of upstate New York still resent immigrants.
Just around the corner from the Himalaya restaurant, 27-year-old Wendell Davenport is standing by his pickup truck.
He said his issue is with people who are undocumented, and he's been that guy who shouts down immigrants saying things like go back to your country from his truck window.
I will not lie about that.
I have done that because my old boss hired illegals and fired half of his crew, so I was one of the crew.
Davenport also said he doesn't like the idea of people moving to the U.S.
only to send money back home.
Dorjee said even though he's a citizen, he's still afraid of getting harassed.
But he's now convinced that, at least in this city, the people who believe in the love and acceptance far outnumber those who are hateful.
For NPR News, I'm Zach Hirsch in Plattsburgh, N.Y.
The United Nations is scheduled to vote tomorrow on the makeup of a new human rights council.
It will be responsible to protect and promote human rights around the world.
The United States has no chance of a seat at the table.
The Bush administration decided to sit out the first election saying it wanted a stronger organization.
Some human rights groups criticize that decision saying the U.S.
will miss out on the opportunity to shape the new council during its first year.
The new human rights council replaces the former Commission on Human Rights.
That body was criticized for including countries like Sudan and Zimbabwe, some of the world's worst human rights violators, as members.
New rules and restrictions for membership are meant to keep offenders off the council and give the U.N.
better enforcement mechanisms.
Gay McDougall advised the U.N.
in testimony last year leading to the new human rights council.
She's a U.N.
independent expert on minority rights and past executive director of Global Rights, which is a human rights organization.
She's with us now by phone from New York.
And thanks very much for being with us today.
Well, thank you.
The U.N.
eventually had to scrap the Commission on Human Rights.
What makes this new human rights council better and different?

Well, the expectation is that first of all it should have a membership of states that have promised to uphold the standards of human rights; that have had to pass a higher threshold for obtaining membership.
Now a majority of states in the general assembly have to vote in favor of each and every member of the council.
Before the membership was based on sort of a closed regional slate of country candidates.
That left no room for evaluating the records of the members or of substituting other nominees.
So first of all, you know, the hope is that we'll have new countries that are on it because they believe in human rights and because they've pledged to have good records themselves.
It will be open to a vote - by a serious vote - by a larger number of the world's countries.
And we hope that, you know, that's going to create a new ethos, a new political culture, if you will, on the council that will make it more effective than the commission was.
It sounds like a lot will depend on who gets elected tomorrow and what choices are made come June when the new members will go ahead and meet for the first time.
That's absolutely right.
The council will have 47 seats on it, and that's a few less than the commission.
But there's 65 countries running for those 47 states.

And as I said, there's now been posted on various Web sites, compilations of records of their voting patterns in the past; their accession to the sort of core human rights treaties; a whole, you know, list of ways to evaluate whether or not they are good governments that are good citizens of the world, in terms of their human rights records.
At this point, even the Bush administration, which as we mentioned earlier, has been critical of this new body, is, though, offering political and financial support.
Do you think the controversy's dying away a little bit?
Well, I hope the controversy is dying away.
But I think that many of us in the human rights world are disappointed that the United States voted against the council and is, indeed, not putting itself up for election.
Well, I guess there is the possibility, and we have to consider it, that the new human rights council could suffer the same fate as the old one.
You know, of course it could.
But everything depends on the 47 member states that take their seats in June when the council is inaugurated and their commitment to, you know, integrity of the process.
They are now going to have the ability to rethink many of the procedures, to rethink the agenda in many ways, and to address whatever deficiencies existed in council.
And there were definitely deficiencies.
But I just want to point out that there were also great successes for the human rights community of great significance over the past, you know, three or four decades with the commission.
So we're all hoping that they're not going to throw out the baby with the bath water and that important aspects of how the commission operated are going to be saved into this new council.
Gay McDougall, thanks very much for being with us today.
Thank you.
And you're listening to TALK OF THE NATION, which is coming to you from NPR News.

In order to get this done, Obama would have had to engage on three levels in a very deep and sustained way: on the level of public messaging; on the level of policy to really get down in the weeds, pick a bill, a specific set of proposals and drive it; and then on the level of good, old-fashioned politicking.
And he didn't do any of those three things.
: It's interesting that you talk about the public image part because there was a little bit of an attempt, anyway, at branding.
Sometimes you heard energy legislation connected with green jobs.
Other times you heard it described as energy legislation instead of climate change.
And the only thing that really seemed to stick was energy tax, and that was from the GOP and Republicans and folks who were against what the Democrats were pushing for.
Well, nobody in support of the bill had the same kind of megaphone that the opponents had other than Obama.
The green groups and the senators who were pushing climate action, they can't compete with Rush Limbaugh and Glenn Beck, if you will.
: But do they have the same cohesiveness of mission even?
No.
No, they don't.
: I mean, doesn't energy and climate change and all these different things, don't they mean different things even to these groups?
If they had started negotiating that a year ago, they might have gotten it done, but they held out for an economy-wide cap until it was too late, and they ended up with nothing.
